textBoxes.text: Title: Ben Eater 8-bit CPU

Description: A working recreation of Ben Eater's SAP-1 breadboard computer - one breadboard per module (clock, program counter, RAM, registers, ALU, microcoded control, output). Single-step the clock and watch a real program run.

BEN EATER 8-BIT CPU (the SAP-1: "Simple As Possible") Each breadboard below is ONE module, exactly like the separate boards in Ben Eater's video series. Read them top-to-bottom: that is the path data takes through the machine. The right-hand board of each row is that module's own LED readout. THE BUS — the shared 8-bit highway Every module hangs off ONE common set of 8 wires. The golden rule: only ONE module may DRIVE the bus at a time, but any number may LISTEN and latch from it. The Control Logic enforces this by asserting exactly one '…-out' (output-enable) signal per clock step. HOW AN INSTRUCTION RUNS — fetch, decode, execute Every instruction starts with the same two fetch steps: T0 CO + MI : Program Counter -> bus -> Memory Address Register (point RAM at the next instruction) T1 RO + II + CE : RAM -> bus -> Instruction Register; counter + 1 (read the instruction, advance the counter) T2+ the opcode (top nibble of the IR) plus the step number address the Control ROMs, which fire the signals that carry out the instruction. CONTROL SIGNAL LEGEND CO/CE program counter out / count up J jump-load the PC MI memory-address-register in RO/RI RAM out / in II instruction in IO instruction operand out AI/AO A register in / out BI B register in EO ALU out SU subtract FI flags in OI output in HLT halt the clock Tip: set the clock to single-step and watch one signal light up per edge.

CLOCK The clock is the heartbeat: it ticks once per step and nothing else moves except on its edge. That single shared tick is what keeps every module in step so the machine acts as one computer instead of 12 loose chips.

CLOCK — the heartbeat Nothing in the CPU moves except on a clock edge, so everything stays in lock-step. In Ben's build a 555 timer (astable) makes the square wave, a 74x08 AND gate + 74x04 inverter let the HALT signal freeze the clock, and a second 555 gives a manual single-step button. Here a built-in 1 Hz clock source drives the CLK net directly (the 555/AND/ INV chips are placed for visual fidelity). Single-step it to walk the machine one micro-step at a time, or free-run it to watch a program go. Drives CLK -> every register, the program counter, and the step counter.
RESET Pressing reset pulls every register and both counters back to zero. The whole computer restarts cleanly from the first instruction.

RESET — clear the machine Press the button to pull the RST line LOW. Every register's CLR input and the program counter / step counter clear to zero, so execution restarts from address 0. Use it to re-run the demo program from the top.

PROGRAM COUNTER Holds the address of the next instruction and counts up by one each fetch, so the program runs in order. It puts that address on the bus when asked, and can be loaded from the bus to perform a jump.

PROGRAM COUNTER — remembers which instruction is next A 74x161 4-bit binary counter holds an address 0-15. CE (count-enable): +1 each fetch, so the program runs in order. J (jump): load a new address from the bus -> that is a JMP. A 74x245 tri-state buffer gates the count onto the bus only when CO is active, so the PC stays off the bus the rest of the time. Green LEDs show the current address being executed.
PROGRAM COUNTER - value (LEDs)
MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER Latches an address off the bus and hands it to RAM, telling memory which byte to read or write. It is how the CPU points at the instruction or data it wants next.

MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER — points RAM at one word A single 74x173 latches the low 4 bits of the bus when MI is asserted. Those 4 outputs become RAM's address lines (via the 74x157 address mux inside the RAM module). The on-board DIP switches let you dial an address by hand for loading a program. Amber LEDs show the selected address.
MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER - value (LEDs)
RANDOM ACCESS MEMORY (16x8) Stores the 16-byte program and its data. It reads the byte at the MAR's address onto the bus, or writes the bus back into that address, so it is the computer's memory.

RANDOM ACCESS MEMORY (16 x 8) — where the program and data live Two 74x189 (16 x 4 static RAM) chips give 16 bytes of memory. A 74x157 mux picks the address source: the running program (from the MAR) or the manual DIP switches for programming. NOTE the 74x189 outputs are INVERTED, so a 74x245 buffer re-inverts and gates the byte onto the bus when RO is active. Writing the bus into RAM (WE) happens when RI is asserted. Pre-loaded with a small program that counts upward on the output display.

INSTRUCTION REGISTER Latches the current instruction byte from the bus. Its top half (the opcode) tells the Control Logic what to do; its bottom half (the operand) can be put back on the bus as an address or value.

INSTRUCTION REGISTER — holds the instruction being executed Two 74x173s latch a whole byte from the bus when II is asserted. Top nibble = OPCODE -> sent straight to the Control Logic to decide what this instruction should do. Bottom nibble = OPERAND (an address or small value); a 74x245 puts it back on the bus when IO is active (e.g. LDA's address). Red LEDs show the latched instruction byte.
INSTRUCTION REGISTER - value (LEDs)

CONTROL LOGIC The brain: stored microcode reads the opcode and step number and fires the right control signals every tick. It decides which module drives the bus and which latches from it, turning a byte into a sequence of actions.

CONTROL LOGIC — the conductor that drives every other module A 74x161 step counter walks through micro-steps T0..T5; a 74x138 decodes the step number. Two 28C16 EEPROMs hold the MICROCODE: they are addressed by { opcode, step, carry-flag, zero-flag } and output the 16 control signals (MI, RO, II, AI, EO, SU, …) that tell every module what to do on THIS clock edge. This is the table that turns a byte into a sequence of actions — the real 'brain' of the CPU.
A REGISTER The accumulator: holds one operand for the ALU and catches most results. It latches a value from the bus and can drive that value back onto the bus.

A REGISTER — the accumulator Two 74x173s latch a byte from the bus on AI. Its value is ALWAYS fed to the ALU as the 'A' operand, and a 74x245 buffer can drive it back onto the bus when AO is active. Almost every result of a computation ends up here. Red LEDs show its contents.
A REGISTER - value (LEDs)

ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT Adds or subtracts the A and B registers and puts the result on the bus. It also reports whether the result carried or came out zero, which is what lets the computer make decisions.

ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT — does the math Two 74x283 4-bit adders compute A + B. Two 74x86 XOR banks conditionally flip every bit of B when SU is set: B XOR 1 then + 1 (carry-in) is exactly two's-complement -B, so the SAME adder also SUBTRACTS. A 74x32 OR tree checks for an all-zero result (the Zero flag). A 74x245 drives the result onto the bus when EO is active. Produces the Carry (CF) and Zero (ZF) flags. Yellow LEDs show the live sum.
ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT - value (LEDs)
B REGISTER Holds the second number for the ALU. It latches a value from the bus and feeds it straight to the adder, so A+B (or A-B) can be computed.

B REGISTER — the ALU's second operand Two 74x173s latch a byte from the bus on BI. Its value is permanently wired to the ALU's 'B' input. Unlike A, the classic SAP-1 B register has no bus- output buffer — it only ever feeds the adder. Red LEDs show its contents.
B REGISTER - value (LEDs)
FLAGS REGISTER Remembers whether the last ALU result carried or was zero. The Control Logic reads these bits so jumps can be conditional - branching only when carry or zero is set.

FLAGS REGISTER — remembers the status of the last result A 74x173 latches two bits from the ALU when FI is asserted: CF (carry): the add overflowed past 8 bits. ZF (zero): the result was exactly zero. The Control Logic reads these to make conditional jumps: JC = jump if carry, JZ = jump if zero. That is how the CPU makes decisions.
FLAGS REGISTER - value (LEDs)
OUTPUT Latches a byte from the bus and shows it as a decimal number on the 7-segment displays. It is the computer's only way to show a result to you.

OUTPUT REGISTER — the display A 74x377 latches a byte from the bus when OI is asserted. A 28C16 EEPROM is a decode ROM that converts the byte into 7-segment patterns; a 74x107 + 74x139 scan the four digits. The four 7-segment displays show the value in decimal. This is the only way a program can 'print' a result.
